title: RFC 9000 Address-Validation Token Emission Architecture
purpose: Describe how the connection runtime issues server-only NEW_TOKEN frames after validating a new client address, retains per-address emission state, and keeps repeated validation transitions from spamming duplicate emissions.
design_summary:
The runtime owns a server-only emission table keyed by remote address.
Path-validation success creates or refreshes a pending token record, then attempts to flush it through the existing 1-RTT application send path once the connection is active and protected packet material exists.
NEW_TOKEN formatting stays internal to the runtime and uses the existing frame codec; repeated validation transitions for the same address reuse the stored emission record instead of creating duplicate frames.
risks: Token emission must remain server-only so client-role validation events do not produce NEW_TOKEN traffic.
A validated path that is not yet active may need to defer emission until the runtime can send a protected 1-RTT packet.
Repeated validation transitions must not bypass the emission table and create duplicate NEW_TOKEN frames.

supplemental_sections.heading: Emission Model
supplemental_sections.content:
Each validated remote address owns one emission record.
A new record carries opaque token bytes and starts in a pending state.
Once the runtime can send a protected application packet, the record is flushed and then stays remembered so later validation transitions for the same address do not emit another NEW_TOKEN frame.
